segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black screen before an upbeat electronic music track begins, accompanying the transition to a man standing behind a table filled with computer hardware boxes against a vibrant blue background. Wearing a dark t-shirt, he speaks directly to the camera, introducing himself and the 'Skydive channel,' initially stating they will build the 'best $3,000 gaming PC in 2023.' An off-screen voice then interjects, questioning, 'Wait, I thought you said 2000,' followed by a cartoonish 'boing' sound and a 'YAY!' sound effect. The man corrects himself, confirming they are building the 'best $2,000 gaming PC in 2023.' He gestures toward the components, which include a Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard, an AMD Ryzen 7 CPU, an ASUS ROG Strix GeForce RTX 4090, a Corsair H100i RGB Platinum liquid cooler, a Seasonic PRIME 850W power supply, and a white PC case wrapped in plastic. He picks up the RTX 4090 box, then an AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX box, placing them back down as he explains that this build offers 'the best of the best' without excessive spending. He elaborates that the CPU and GPU in this $2,000 gaming PC will 'crush it' for 4K gaming, even with ray tracing, and perform excellently for content creation and game streaming. The scene briefly cuts to a fully assembled white PC with RGB lighting, showcasing a clean, modern build with a liquid cooler and multiple fans, before returning to the man. Brief gameplay footage is then shown: first, a racing game with a white car on a rainy track, followed by a first-person shooter in a desert environment. The video concludes with the man back at the table, smiling and gesturing toward the components, as on-screen text briefly displays 'be building' and '2000', reinforcing the PC's versatility.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand using a yellow screwdriver to secure components inside a white PC case, accompanied by the text 'Build guide'. The scene transitions to a computer screen displaying the installation of AMD Software Adrenalin Edition, with 'Windows & Drivers installation' overlaid. This is followed by dynamic in-game footage of a racing game and a first-person shooter, labeled 'Gaming Benchmarks'. Throughout these initial visuals, a man with a clear, enthusiastic male voice explains that he will "show you guys how to put together this PC step-by-step, the parts going inside of it, how to install Windows, drivers, and then the 4K gaming benchmarks," concluding with his "final thoughts on if you should build this $2000 computer." The scene then shifts to the Man, a young man with dark hair wearing a dark t-shirt, standing behind a table laden with various computer components, including the Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard Box, AMD Ryzen 7 CPU Box, ASUS ROG Strix GeForce RTX 4090 Box, Corsair H100i RGB Platinum Box, Seasonic PRIME 850W Power Supply Box, and a White PC Case wrapped in plastic. He gestures toward the components while speaking, and the text 'Links in the description' appears. He continues, stating that "links to all of the parts I use in this build guide can be found in the description below," encouraging viewers to "price check anything" or "check out the parts yourself." He then humorously adds, "to build this $2000 computer, all you need is a banana, scissors, and an 8 gigabyte or bigger USB flash drive." The Man picks up the AMD Ryzen 7 CPU Box and uses scissors to open it, with a distinct cutting sound, before holding up the CPU in its protective packaging. The text 'CPU: Ryzen 7 7800X3D link in description' is displayed. He elaborates on the CPU, identifying it as the "AMD Ryzen 7 7800X3D," and confidently declares it "the best gaming CPU you can build, or you can get in 2023," emphasizing that "you don't need more than 8 cores for even a high-end gaming PC." The background features a solid blue with vertical white light tubes, creating a clean, tech-focused atmosphere, and an Assembled White PC is visible in the background during the CPU showcase.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an upbeat electronic music track as a close-up reveals a Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard inside a white PC case, illuminated by vibrant blue and purple RGB lighting, highlighting its 'B650 AORUS' branding and 'Socket AM5' text. The camera pans across the motherboard, showcasing its sleek white and silver design, including the 'TEAM UP. FIGHT ON.' slogan. A young man with dark hair, wearing a dark t-shirt, then appears behind a table against a blue-lit backdrop, stating, "That's pretty cool. This is an all-white B650 motherboard. It's going to be more than enough for our 7800X3D and wow, this is a beauty." He unboxes the Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard from its white box, revealing accessories like a Wi-Fi antenna, a "handy little front IO adapter," and a QR code sheet for the manual, which he describes. He then holds up a small black component, the 7800X3D CPU, and displays it with the motherboard, announcing, "Now let's go ahead and install the 7800X3D onto our motherboard." With meticulous precision, his hands are shown carefully installing the CPU into the motherboard’s socket, securing it with a retention mechanism. He explains the need to "unscrew these brackets" and notes that "since this is a 7800X3D... it doesn't come with a stock cooler." He proceeds to fasten the mounting screws for a water cooler, specifically mentioning an "Enermax AIO," using a screwdriver around the heatsink, maintaining focus on the clean, white aesthetic of the components and the careful handling of each part.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a white G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM stick being installed into a motherboard, set against a sleek blue-lit background. A young man with dark hair, wearing a dark t-shirt, then appears behind a component-laden table, speaking directly to the camera with clear, professional speech. He explains his choice of RAM, noting that "pretty much anything is going to be universal with this Ryzen system," but specifically chose this RAM because "it's white" and boasts a "speed of 6000 MHz with a CAS latency of 30," which is "really nice." He reveals he acquired the RAM kit for "about 100 bucks," considering it a "pretty nice deal," and advises against spending more for "minimal FPS gains," concluding they are "making the most of it with our budget with these two sticks of RAM." He then transitions to discussing SSDs, holding up a silver M.2 SSD, identified on-screen as the 'T-Force Cardea A440 Pro 2TB'. He places it on the table, picks up and examines a transparent plastic case component, and then displays multiple M.2 SSDs side by side. The man introduces the "Team Group T-Force Cardera A440 Pro" as a "PCI Gen 4.0 SSD, 2 terabytes," comparing its value to the RAM by stating it's "as expensive as you'd want to go for an M.2 SSD without really overspending," while still being a "high quality, premium M.2 SSD." A close-up shot shows the T-Force SSD being installed into the Gigabyte AORUS B650 motherboard, with on-screen text confirming 'M.2 SSD: T-Force Cardea A440 Pro'. The man returns to the table, holding a small component in a clear plastic bag, continuing his explanation about "read and write speed." The scene maintains a professional, tech-focused atmosphere with bright vertical LED strips casting a cool blue glow, and the table remains cluttered with various parts and tools.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a young man, dark-haired and wearing a dark t-shirt, seated behind a table against a vibrant blue backdrop illuminated by vertical white light tubes. The man then prepares for M.2 SSD installation, stating, "And now for M.2 SSD, we're going to change the bits on our screwdriver here to a smaller one, that being a Phillips head one bit, because we are dealing with smaller screws." His movements are deliberate and confident throughout the technical build process, maintaining the cool blue lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with the Man, a young man with dark hair wearing a dark t-shirt, seated behind a table against a vibrant blue backdrop, introducing the PC build process in a clear, instructional tone. He gestures toward the open White PC Case, identified as the Montech King 95 Pro, which is positioned prominently, revealing its internal structure with two white fans already installed. He explains that he has "unscrewed" parts and can "press this out, take it out" to gain "access to the inside of the PC case." The Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard Box is visible to his left. The Man then opens the case further, revealing the motherboard installed inside, with the CPU socket exposed and the AORUS branding clearly visible. He notes that "all the standoffs [are] pre-installed for our motherboard" and, referring to the large size of the "King 95" case, states, "you can literally just drop it in." He describes his method of aligning the motherboard by lining up "the rear IO here with the opening on the back of the King 95," which allows him to "screw in our motherboard screws." He points out that these screws are visible "on screen" and are described as "18 pieces" found in "the box." He advises viewers to "check out the case manual" for specific screw details. The camera zooms in for a top-down view as he begins installing components, first securing the CPU Cooler with a screwdriver, aligning it carefully over the CPU socket. He describes his technique of screwing in one screw without tightening it, just making "light contact with the motherboard," before moving to a corner. He then proceeds to install the G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM into the slots, followed by the M.2 SSD: T-Force Cardea A440 Pro, which is inserted into the M.2 slot on the motherboard. Throughout the process, he briefly references a User Manual. The internal blue glow of the Montech King 95 Pro PC Case highlights the clean, white interior and the precision of the build, with the Man’s hands steady and deliberate, creating a professional and informative atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with a close-up inside the open White PC Case, illuminated by vibrant blue RGB lighting. The Man's voice provides instruction, stating, "do that for the rest of the screws here on the motherboard, just lightly tighten them," and then guides the viewer through a "criss-cross" tightening method to "put equal pressure on all sides of the motherboard so there's no motherboard flex." The scene transitions to a wider shot of the Man, wearing a dark t-shirt, seated at a table with the White PC Case positioned to his right. He holds up the Seasonic PRIME 850W Power Supply Box, which is clearly labeled 'CENTURY G5 850W' and features '80 PLUS GOLD' certification. The Man explicitly identifies it as "the Montech Century G5 850 watt, 80 plus gold," explaining that it "will do the job" and is "more than enough" in terms of quality, despite its price point. He further specifies its suitability, noting it's "plenty for a 7800 X3D and a 7900 XTX" as he rotates the box to display its technical specifications before placing it back on the table. He concludes, "All right, let's unbox this," gesturing toward the power supply box and the PC case, all set against a clean blue background accented by vertical white light tubes.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment focuses on the meticulous process of connecting modular power cables inside the brightly lit White PC Case: Montech King 95 Pro, which showcases blue RGB accents from its Montech Fans. A Man's hand, guided by his clear, instructional male voice, begins by stating, "Let's go ahead and plug in our power connectors, starting with the CPU power connector." He carefully routes a black, flat, sleeved Modular Cable, labeled 'CPU', explaining it's an "8-pin by 4-pin setup" that goes "on the top left of your motherboard." He highlights the latch and notch for proper alignment, reassuring viewers, "this will only go in one way. You will not mess this up," as he precisely plugs it into the 4-pin CPU power connector on the Gigabyte AORUS B650 Motherboard. Upon connection, he confirms, "There we go, we got one in there." He then explains the cable's configuration, noting that splitting it provides "two separate four-pin connectors," but only one is needed for the "last connector," emphasizing, "Make sure to press it in all the way, or else you're going to run into issues." The hand then moves to connect a second Modular Cable, labeled 'PCIe', to the motherboard's PCIe power header, with the Man instructing to "Line the two up, bring this around. Give it a firm press, make sure it's all the way in there." The motherboard, featuring a white PCB with 'AORUS' branding, an exposed AMD Ryzen CPU socket, 'F_PANEL' header, and G.SKILL Ripjaws S5 DDR5 RAM sticks, is clearly visible throughout, all while the Man prepares to connect "my two PCIe power..." The entire process highlights precision and neatness, ensuring tidy cable management for optimal airflow and aesthetics.

segments.integrated_caption: The video transitions to the final PC setup, where the Man, now in a white t-shirt, is seated at a black desk with a colorful mousepad, black keyboard, and monitor. He begins by instructing viewers to ensure their "keyboard and your mouse are plugged into your computer," and that a "display cable" runs "from the monitor to where your graphics card is in your PC," emphasizing not to plug it into the motherboard. He then advises to "supply power to your monitor, so it actually turns on." Visually, he connects a black power cable to the rear of the Assembled White PC, a Montech King 95 Pro with internal RGB lighting and a transparent side panel, explaining to plug it into "the back of the computer where the power supply is," specifically the 'TOUGHPower GF3 750W' PSU. He instructs to "flip the switch from the zero to the one" on the PC, then takes a small black USB device, identifying it as a "Windows bootable media device," and plugs it into the front USB port, noting he's putting it "in the front because that's what I feel like doing." After pressing the power button, the monitor displays the AMI BIOS screen, prompting the Man to exclaim "Ooh!" and guide the viewer, saying "If you see something like this, just go ahead and proceed." He explains they will "reset the FTPM" by pressing 'Y' on the keyboard. As the PC boots into Windows Setup, showing 'Setup is starting' and prompting for Windows activation, the Man points at the screen, indicating the next steps, stating, "There's a little circle here, and now let's go ahead and install Windows," concluding with "All right, let's go ahead and install Windows. So hit install now." The well-lit, modern environment maintains focus on the Man's instructional interaction with the PC setup.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a male voice guiding the Windows 11 installation on a large monitor, instructing the viewer to either enter a product key or skip it, which he does. He then directs the selection of the Windows 11 Home 64-bit version and proceeds through installation type, confirming the drive. The instructional tone continues as he prompts the user to choose their country (United States is visible), keyboard layout (US), and language. A hand gestures towards the screen, emphasizing these selections. The narrator then explains connecting to the internet, stating "I'm going to go ahead and connect to my internet here," before hitting "next." He advises on naming the device but opts to skip it, then moves to signing into a Microsoft account, explaining how to use a previous account from "Xbox Live" or a current computer, or to create a new one. The screen visually progresses through naming the device, adding a Microsoft account, and showing an option to restore data from 'TheGigaPC'. The instructor then guides to "click more options" and "set up as a new PC" if presented, concluding with the instruction to "create the login we're going to use." The monitor, in a well-lit room, clearly displays the Microsoft logo, Wi-Fi symbol, and various app icons like Edge, Xbox, and OneDrive throughout the instructional setup process, including a progress indicator for PIN setup, though the PIN selection itself isn't explicitly narrated in this minute.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a male voice narrating the Windows 11 setup process, advising to 'tick them to off' for unnecessary features and to 'skip or decline' extra offers for PC games. The visual transitions from the setup to the Windows 11 desktop environment, where a hand gestures on the black monitor. The narrator announces, 'All right, we are on the Windows home screen,' and states the next step is to 'install graphics card drivers and our chipset drivers.' The hand then opens a web browser, and the narrator instructs to 'type in Radeon drivers' and 'click on AMD Drivers and Support.' The browser navigates to the AMD Drivers & Support Page, where the narrator explains they already know the graphics card is the '7900 XTX.' The hand selects the AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX, and the narrator guides the viewer to find the 'Windows 11 version' and either 'auto-detect and install' or use the 'Adrenalin Edition,' initiating the download. While the graphics drivers download, the narrator instructs to 'type in the name of our motherboard,' which is the 'Aorus B650 Elite AX Ice.' The hand searches for 'gigabyte b650 elite ax ice drivers,' leading to the Gigabyte Support Page, which the narrator identifies as the 'motherboard product page for it,' ready for further driver downloads.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video transitions from the Windows 11 setup process to the desktop environment, where a hand interacts with the screen to demonstrate features like file syncing between devices. The user then opens a web browser and navigates to the AMD website to download drivers for an AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX graphics card, selecting the Windows 11 64-bit version and initiating the download. After a brief pop-up for AMD newsletter subscription, the user returns to the browser and searches for 'gigabyte b650 elite ax ice drivers', navigating to the Gigabyte support page to download the latest motherboard drivers. The sequence highlights the post-build software setup phase, focusing on driver installation for optimal system performance, with clear on-screen text and user interaction guiding the process.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a black monitor displaying the Gigabyte support page for the B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE motherboard. A male voice instructs the viewer to "Click on support," then "click on utility," as a human hand, appearing from the left, gestures and navigates the web browser to the driver download section. The speaker highlights the "Gigabyte Control Center full installation package," explaining its benefit over individually downloading each driver. The hand points to and selects the download, prompting a pop-up window for permission, which the speaker instructs to "Click yes." The download begins, and the user proceeds to open the downloaded file. The speaker then guides the installation of the "graphics card driver installation software," referring to the AMD Software: Adrenalin Edition installer that appears with its red and black design, showing progress and a welcome screen. The speaker says, "There you go, there's our graphics card install, let this run and let it do its thing." While that runs, the speaker instructs to "open up the Gigabyte Control Center file" and "extract all of this," as the hand interacts with the screen. After extracting, the speaker says "let's run" and "hit yes." The installation window for the Gigabyte Control Center then appears, displaying a license agreement against the Windows 11 default blue abstract wallpaper, with the taskbar visible. The speaker notes that "our graphics card driver is finished installing, but we're not going to restart just yet," instructing to "hit finish" as the Gigabyte Control Center installation is "finishing up." A black cookie consent banner remains visible at the bottom of the browser window throughout the process.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Monitor displaying the Gigabyte Control Center software loading, as a Man's clear, instructional voice explains they're checking "what software is currently available." A Hand enters, gesturing as the software transitions to the 'Available Updates' page, listing drivers like Norton Internet Security, Realtek audio, AMD Chipset Driver, and AMD Ryzen Master. The Man's voice guides the viewer, stating they "don't need Norton Internet Security" or "AMD Ryzen Master," but do need "AMD Chipset drivers," "Realtek audio drivers," and "the WiFi driver for our WiFi stick and Bluetooth." The Hand points to specific updates, indicating selections, before clicking 'Install' as the Man instructs to "let this go ahead and run." The screen then shows a 'Restarting' message, and the Man announces they'll "restart the computer" to do "one last thing in the BIOS." After the restart, the BIOS Interface appears, showing the AORUS Easy Mode with system information. The Man's voice instructs to "press the delete key on your keyboard when the logo of the motherboard comes up" to enter the BIOS, and then to "go ahead and enable your XMP profile for your RAM," before clicking save and exit, declaring "you are done." The system boots into Windows, transitioning to vibrant Racing Game Footage of a white race car on a rainy track with performance metrics. A small circular inset appears in the top right, showing the Man, now in a white t-shirt, holding a small black USB device and observing the gameplay, as his voice excitedly asks, "Alright, what about Forza Motorsport?" The Desk Setup and Assembled White PC remain consistent, now fully operational and displaying the game.

The hand points to specific updates, indicating selections, before clicking the 'Install' button. The screen then shows a 'Restarting' message as the system reboots. After the restart, the BIOS interface appears, showing the AORUS Easy Mode with system information such as CPU frequency, memory frequency, and temperature. The hand navigates the BIOS, enabling XMP/EXPO for the G.SKILL DDR5 RAM, and then saves and exits. The system boots into Windows, and the video transitions to gameplay footage of a racing game, showing a white race car on a rainy track with performance metrics displayed. A small circular inset appears in the top right corner, showing the Man, now wearing a white t-shirt, seated at the desk, holding a small black USB device, and observing the gameplay. The environment remains consistent with the previous segment, featuring the same desk setup with a black keyboard, mouse, and monitor, and the Assembled White PC to the right, now fully operational and displaying the game.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a third-person rear-view perspective of a white Bentley race car, branded 'Mobil 1 SERVICE CENTRE', navigating a wet, rain-slicked Hockenheim racetrack in a racing game, a track the player notes "just got released." Raindrops streak across the screen, reducing visibility and creating reflective surfaces, as the player comments, "the game was like, 'yo, let's put you on rainy graphics,' 'cause why not?" The car is positioned behind a red Corvette and a black race car, with several other colorful vehicles ahead. The environment is foggy and overcast, with trackside barriers, grandstands, and distant trees barely visible. In the top right, a young man with dark hair, wearing a white t-shirt and earphones, is seated at a desk, observing the gameplay with focused attention. He speaks throughout, describing how they are "running this game completely maxed out at 4K FPS," noting the FPS display is "so small" his "editor's gonna have to enlarge that." He identifies his car as the "Boatley" (Bentley), praising its "great brakes" and his "good connection with the brakes." On-screen HUD elements display FPS fluctuating between 92 and 113, GPU temperature around 65–67°C, and the current lap timer progressing from 00:00.00 to 00:57.00, showing 'LAP 1/2'. As the player accelerates through straightaways and navigates turns, guided by blue directional arrows, he expresses frustration, "Why am I in first gear? No, no, on a wet circuit. Nope," and acknowledges, "cars kind of all over the place." He also notes difficulty hearing the car's need to "upshift." Notifications for '+10 EXP Overtake' and '+200 CP Level Up Reward' appear as he successfully overtakes a red car and a black Red Bull car. The track features overhead banners like 'SPLI SECOND' and 'REVENOL', and the car passes under a bridge structure. The gameplay is smooth, with consistent frame rates and dynamic weather effects, accompanied by the sounds of racing engines, tire squeals, and rain. The man in the inset remains engaged, occasionally gesturing with his right hand (pointing, thumbs-up), reacting subtly to the race’s progression, and even laughing at one point while anticipating a corner. The in-game car maintains a steady position in the pack, approaching the end of the first lap, as the man's commentary cuts off.

entity_library.Gigabyte Support Page: A webpage with a clean white layout, displaying the 'B650 AORUS ELITE AX ICE' product page, including a driver download section with version, size, date, and download links for various components, such as the GIGABYTE Control Center Full Installation Package, which the male speaker specifically names and instructs the user to download.
entity_library.AMD Software: Adrenalin Edition Installe: A pop-up window with a red and black design, displaying the AMD Software: Adrenalin Edition installer, showing progress and a welcome screen, with options to 'Install' or 'Cancel', which the male speaker refers to as the "graphics card driver installation software" and guides the user to install.
entity_library.GIGABYTE CONTROL CENTER: An application window showing the 'Available Updates' page with a list of drivers and software, including Norton Internet Security, Realtek drivers, AMD Chipset Driver, and AMD Ryzen Master, with 'Install' and 'Exit' buttons at the bottom, whose contents are verbally analyzed by the Man, who specifies which updates are necessary.

entity_library.BIOS Interface: The AORUS Easy Mode BIOS screen displaying system information such as CPU frequency (4204.55 MHz), memory frequency (4890.36 MT/s), CPU temperature (28.0°C), and RAM status, with options to enable XMP/EXPO and save settings, where the Man's voice guides the user to enable the XMP profile for RAM and then save and exit.
entity_library.Starfield: In-game footage from the game 'Starfield', showing the settings menu with display options such as 'Upscaling' (FSR2), 'Enable VRS', and 'Render Resolution Scale' (set to 100%), with FPS fluctuating between 165 and 154, and the Man adjusting settings while reacting in the inset. The Man's detailed audio commentary focuses entirely on this game, praising its 'stunning' visuals at '4K' and 'Ultra settings' and discussing specific graphic options like 'sharpening and film grain' and 'Depth of field'.
entity_library.Subtitles: Text at the bottom of the screen displaying in-game dialogue and advertisements, such as 'Reliant Medical: It's a dangerous galaxy. entity_library.HUD: A heads-up display (HUD) showing game metrics such as health, weapon status, and kill notifications, accompanied by distinct UI sounds during menu navigation and combat, with performance metrics (FPS, GPU temperature) also displayed in the top left corner.
entity_library.ASRock TAICHI WHITE Radeon RX 7900 XTX G: A large white box with 'ASRock TAICHI WHITE GRAPHICS CARD' and 'AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX' branding, featuring a red '24GB' label, held by a hand in front of the Assembled White PC. entity_library.Enermax Aquafusion ADX: A liquid cooling system with a red-lit reservoir/pump component and white tubing, installed inside the case, visible during close-ups of the internal components.
